result:
The market by the river wakes like something remembering itself. Canvas awnings snap, poles thud into their holsters, the first kettles hiss. A woman lays down a velvet cloth and pours antique brooches over it like a slow glittering rain. A man with a wide-brimmed hat tests the wobble on a three-legged stool. There is a shared agreement, unspoken, that everything is for sale because everything has already been owned by someone else who thought it might be permanent.

The river looks like common metal under a gray sky. When a breeze comes up from it, it carries cold and the smell of damp leaves. A child in a red coat reaches for a stack of books taller than him and chooses one with an elephant on the cover. He tucks it under his arm with the solemnity of someone guarding a secret.

At the coffee cart a barista with inked forearms draws a tiny fern into the milk. The cup warms my palms through thin gloves. I have not slept well, but here I am among rust and linen and jars of honey so clear they look like amber light in glass. The beekeeper says the flavor is different each week because the flowers keep changing their minds. He laughs and holds out a spoon. The honey is clover, high and bright and grassy, and for a second it resets the day.

There is a tray of mismatched cutlery and I fish out a fork with a bone handle. The seller tells me it belonged to his aunt who could peel an apple in one perfect spiral with a paring knife and catch the skin in her mouth. He offers this as proof that the fork has known grace, and I want to believe him. Objects carry stories, or maybe we lay stories over them like table runners and hope they will stay put.

A man in a leather jacket sells records and speaks about them as if they are old friends who moved to other cities but still send letters. He knows which ones will throw their voices across a room and which ones only make sense late at night. He pulls out one with a split spine and says, This one survived a basement flood. The ink bled here, but the song? Untouched. It is a kind of mercy when a thing you love proves itself waterproof.

The sun nudges the clouds aside and everything brightens by a quarter tone. A woman with a woven basket checks the seam on a handmade dress, the way people check the backs of paintings: for truth. A boy flips a switch on a lamp shaped like the moon and it glows like the real thing on washing days—visible at four in the afternoon, a pale coin no one is sure what to do with.

Near the waterline someone sells maps. They are folded and refolded so often they have soft creases like the palms of old hands. There are routes no one takes now, roads decommissioned, names changed. He lays one flat and points to a town once called something else. He says, If you set out with this, you will end up in a place that believes it has never met you. I think of all the times I have arrived late and unrecognizable to my own life.

There is a stand that sells houseplants with little limiting instructions tucked into the soil: East light. Infrequent watering. Talk to me if you can. The woman selling them has soil under her nails and the kindest way of naming difficulties. When a man says he killed his last fern, she scrapes back the dirt from a pot and says, It isn’t a grave until it is. Try again.

A violinist under the arch plays something slow enough to hear the friction of bow on string. A couple sways reflexively, his palm on her shoulder, her head listing toward the music. They are wearing the look of people who have not argued this morning, who have left their house with their small petty inventory of grievances but for now those items are folded in the drawer. I like the way music can postpone a fight. I like thinking about two versions of the day unspooling from here, one sharp and one soft, and how the difference might be nothing more than one more song.

At the stall with the old cameras I hold a square-bodied one to my eye and see the world in a frosted sugar. The vendor tells me it still works if I can find the film. He says that taking a picture with it is like sending a letter to a slower version of yourself. I don’t need another object, but I like the idea of waiting for the world to develop. I imagine cutting the tape on an envelope and finding, inside, proof that we were once standing here with our shoulders squared to the wind and our faces unafraid.

Past noon, the smells grow thicker—fried dough, garlic, the smoke of something grilling. A woman is selling soups in glass jars and I buy one labeled “Tuesday.” I ask what’s in it and she says, You’ll know when you taste it. Later, the label will go into the drawer with all the other labels and train tickets and programs, the small paper reliquaries of days that wanted to be remembered.

I think about the way markets are a rehearsal for leaving with less than you came with. You bring money, an assumption of need, a wish to be surprised. You leave with a fork, a jar, a song caught behind the ribs, a question about maps. You leave with fewer minutes in your pocket. You leave with the knowledge that someone else held these things before you, that your hands are never the first.

Down by the river a cantankerous gull hops on one foot and screams his opinion of everything. The water, indifferent, presses past. On the far bank a crane leans over a construction site like a giant considering a chess move. The skyline is a row of promises still under scaffolding. Somewhere a siren starts and stops, unsure of its own urgency.

When I finish my coffee, I notice the imprint my thumb has left in the foam, a tiny crescent. The cup, the morning, the stalls—it all has the feeling of something that will be called “that time” in a story told later. We are always collecting future pasts, banding them like rings on a tree. I touch the fork in my pocket, the handle smooth from someone else’s life.

On the walk home I pass a woman standing on her stoop holding a potted rosemary up to the sun as if baptizing it. A boy on a scooter lifts one foot and coasts, face tilted to the cold. A bus sighs at a stop, doors opening like a yawn. I think about how many ordinary thresholds I have crossed, how rarely I have known the moment as the moment, and I promise nothing grand, only this: to notice when the light hits the jar on the counter and makes the room into a golden aquarium. To pause at the sink with my hands in soapy water and say, Here I am. To be present for the small exceptional seconds that keep multiplying, even when I think I am not available for them.

At the corner, the wind changes. It smells a little like rain, like pennies left on a windowsill. The sky is a pale animal. Somewhere people are unpinning canvas, counting out change, digging for tape. The river, having said nothing at all, keeps saying nothing as it moves on. I turn my key in the lock and step inside. The soup will be called Tuesday no matter what day it is when I open it. I set the fork in the drawer among its mismatched friends. The day arranges itself on the counter: a map with brown roads like veins, a plant with its leaves folded like hands, a song I can’t name but can hum. I stand for a minute in the doorway, not going anywhere yet, and let it be enough.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
